ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Cantonese almond cream (杏仁糊). Closest match is almond beverage (FOODON_03302596); this is a thicker, cooked preparation distinct from almond milk.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Cantonese almond cream. A00LC (almond-based drink) is the closest defensible anchor. Preparation involves grinding and simmering, producing a thicker product than standard almond milk.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this traditional Cantonese dessert preparation.

main_ingredients: sweet almonds (southern apricot kernels, nan xing)
bitter almonds (northern apricot kernels, bei xing)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Almond Cream is one bowl of approximately 250 g, typically served warm as a dessert or breakfast item.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Almond milk, unsweetened (NDB 45226914) and Almonds, raw (NDB 12061), adapted for traditional Cantonese almond cream preparation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Cantonese almond cream (杏仁糊) exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for raw almonds (NDB 12061) and rice (NDB 20444), diluted and adjusted to reflect the typical 1:4 almond-to-water ratio with added rice and rock sugar used in traditional preparation. Input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are consistent with this adaptation and have been retained with cross-reference to USDA almond nutrient profiles. Saturated fat estimated from USDA almond fatty acid profile (approx. 10% of total fat).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Cantonese almond cream (杏仁糊) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 40 (low) based on analogous almond-based beverage entries and the moderating effect of high fat (7.5 g/100g), protein (3.2 g/100g), and dietary fibre (1.5 g/100g) from almonds on glycemic response. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (40 × 9.8 g carbohydrate per 100g × 2.5) / 100 = 9.8 g. This is consistent with the low-GI classification for almond-based preparations in peer-reviewed literature.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (10 mg/100g) with beneficial potassium (180 mg/100g) and magnesium (45 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Almond Cream is suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its very low sodium content and meaningful potassium and magnesium levels, which support healthy blood pressure regulation; however, caloric density from almond fat should be considered in weight management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~40) moderated by almond fat and protein, but contains added rock sugar contributing 6.5 g sugar per 100g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Almond Cream has a low estimated glycemic index (~40) due to its fat, protein, and fibre content from almonds, making it a relatively blood-sugar-stable dessert option; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request reduced or no added rock sugar and limit portion size to one standard bowl (250 g) to manage carbohydrate intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High monounsaturated fat (~4.7 g/100g), low saturated fat (~0.6 g/100g), zero cholesterol, and significant Vitamin E (4.5 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Almond Cream provides a heart-healthy fat profile dominated by monounsaturated fatty acids and is free of cholesterol and trans fats; its Vitamin E content (4.5 mg/100g) contributes antioxidant protection, making it a suitable occasional dessert for individuals managing cardiovascular disease when prepared with minimal added sugar.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High Vitamin E content (4.5 mg/100g) with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Almond Cream is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as almonds are a rich source of Vitamin E, which has documented antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties; no specific contraindications apply.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Almond Cream (杏仁糊, Xìngrén hú), also known as Almond Tea (杏仁茶), is a classic Cantonese sweet soup (tong sui) originating from Guangdong province, China, and widely enjoyed in Hong Kong and across the Chinese diaspora. It is prepared by soaking and blanching a blend of sweet southern apricot kernels (nan xing) and bitter northern apricot kernels (bei xing), then grinding them together with soaked rice and water into a smooth slurry, which is simmered until it thickens into a silky, creamy consistency. Rock sugar is added to sweeten the final product. The result is a warm, fragrant, ivory-coloured dessert with a distinctly floral, slightly nutty almond aroma and a smooth, velvety texture that is thicker than Western almond milk but lighter than a pudding. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 115 kcal, 3.2 g protein, 7.5 g fat (predominantly heart-healthy monounsaturated fatty acids), 9.8 g carbohydrate, and 1.5 g dietary fibre. It is notably rich in Vitamin E (4.5 mg/100g), magnesium (45 mg/100g), potassium (180 mg/100g), and phosphorus (110 mg/100g), while being very low in sodium (10 mg/100g) and free of cholesterol. In traditional Chinese medicine, it is valued for its lung-moistening and throat-soothing properties, making it a popular home remedy for dry coughs and skin dryness. Clinically, its low glycemic index (~40), low sodium, and favourable fat profile make it a suitable dessert for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes (with reduced sugar), and cardiovascular disease. It is naturally vegan, dairy-free, and gluten-free.
common_user_queries: What is almond cream Chinese dessert?
Is almond cream good for diabetics?
What is Xing Ren Hu made of?
Almond cream vs almond milk difference
Is 杏仁茶 good for cough?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Cantonese almond cream exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central almond and rice entries, consistent with the AI-generated input values (confidence 0.9). GI is an analogous estimate based on almond-based beverage data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the absence of a direct approved-source match for this specific traditional preparation.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central (almonds raw, NDB 12061; white rice cooked, NDB 20444) to reflect traditional Cantonese almond cream preparation; no exact match available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this dish. 2. Saturated fat (0.6 g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (4.7 g/100g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.8 g/100g) estimated from USDA almond fatty acid profile scaled to the diluted preparation. 3. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific cooked preparation (ground almonds simmered with rice and water) is not directly reported as a discrete value in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database as of 2026-04-17; the high water content is implicit in the preparation method but no authoritative per-100g figure is available from an approved source for this traditional dish. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — almonds and rice contain no trans fats by natural composition). 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — this is a plant-based preparation containing no animal products). 6. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_b12_mcg are 0.0 (confirmed negligible/zero per USDA FoodData Central almond profile; almonds contain no vitamin B12, vitamin D, or vitamin C, and negligible vitamin A). 7. GI estimated at 40 (low) from analogous almond-based beverage data; no direct GI measurement for 杏仁糊 found in University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-17. 8. Duplicate portion entry in input (two identical 250g entries) — retained as single 250g standard serving. 9. Input source 'Food Chemistry Journal' (ScienceDirect) is not on the approved source list; replaced with USDA FoodData Central as primary nutrient source per Project Veritas v2 source policy.
